name: AI-accessible internal data
question: AI tools used by the software project team can securely retrieve relevant internal code, documentation, standards, metrics, and logs, so responses are grounded in current project context rather than generic or outdated information.
description: AI-accessible internal data means AI systems used by software teams can draw on relevant internal information rather than relying only on generic model knowledge. In practice, this includes code, architecture information, documentation, style guides, policies, operational metrics, and logs that provide project-specific context. The capability is visible when AI responses reflect current team standards, known system behavior, and approved examples instead of stale or unrelated patterns. It also involves retrieval that supplies focused context for the task rather than large amounts of irrelevant material. Access is handled through appropriate permissions so the AI can use information the requester is allowed to see.

guidance.text: Begin by selecting one high-value workflow where AI answers often need internal context, such as onboarding, debugging, or API usage. Identify the minimum internal sources needed for that workflow, such as current API documentation, coding standards, or representative code examples. Stop relying on isolated prompts that omit project context, and require requests to include specific references until retrieval is available. Measure whether context-rich requests reduce time spent searching for information or verifying AI output.

Strengthen manual context engineering by turning recurring prompts and attached sources into shared, version-controlled context templates. Review the referenced documentation and examples before reuse so outdated or inaccurate material is not passed to AI tools. Prioritize improving the most-used internal documents, because unreliable source material leads to unreliable answers. Track which sources are used most often and where team members still need to assemble context manually.

Move from manual attachment toward an automated retrieval pilot for one clear use case with a measurable benefit. Configure the pilot to retrieve only the specific chunks needed for the request rather than feeding whole documents into the model. Compare outputs against current internal standards, approved code examples, and expected behavior to check whether the context is relevant. Record retrieval success, access-denied cases, errors, and time-to-context so the team can see whether automation is working.

Expand automated access carefully by adding additional trusted data sources only after the pilot shows reliable retrieval and useful responses. Curate indexed code and documentation so deprecated projects, stale snippets, and weak examples do not become patterns for generated output. Enforce least-privilege access so retrieval uses the requester’s permissions rather than a shared elevated account. Monitor source access rates, query outcomes, latency, and the share of prompts that include relevant context.

Keep the capability stable by continuously checking that retrieved context remains current, specific, and aligned with the team’s standards. Remove or quarantine obsolete repositories, documentation, and policies before they can influence AI responses. Review retrieval metrics and verification effort periodically to detect context rot, rising latency, failed queries, or declining trust in outputs. Maintain ownership for critical internal data sources and update access controls as systems, roles, and documentation change.

name: Clear and communicated AI stance
question: To what extent do you agree that team members can readily identify which AI tools and use cases are allowed, which require guardrails, and where to ask policy questions during day-to-day development work?
description: A clear and communicated AI stance means software teams have practical, shared understanding of how AI may be used in their work. It covers which tools are permitted, which uses are prohibited, and which situations require specific controls or guardrails. The stance is visible in daily practice when developers know where to find current guidance, understand how it applies to their role, and can ask questions without relying on guesswork. It reduces uncertainty around AI use by turning broad policy into accessible expectations for development work. It also gives teams a common reference point for safe experimentation, tool selection, and handling sensitive data.

guidance.text: Start by identifying where uncertainty blocks or distorts AI use in daily development work. Create a short inventory of common AI use cases, tools currently used, and questions developers repeatedly raise about permission, data handling, or risk. Stop relying on informal interpretations or hidden individual practices as the main source of guidance. Establish a single place where the current stance, approved tools, and unresolved questions can be collected.

Translate the stance into concrete categories that developers can apply during routine work: prohibited uses, permitted uses with guardrails, and allowed low-risk uses. Add examples that reflect real engineering tasks, such as code generation, debugging, brainstorming, or handling proprietary code and customer data. Make the guidance searchable and easy to find from the places developers already use. Track the questions that still require clarification so the stance can become more specific.

Strengthen communication by discussing the stance in team meetings, onboarding, and developer forums rather than only publishing a document. Confirm that developers understand which AI tools are permitted, which data classifications are allowed for each tool, and how the policy applies to their role. Create a visible feedback channel for requesting clarification or suggesting new tools. Measure recurring questions and policy-page engagement to identify areas where the stance is still unclear.

Improve consistency by involving engineering, security, legal, IT, and product perspectives when the stance is reviewed or updated. Use developer feedback and clarification requests to refine examples, guardrails, and the approved-tool list. Avoid frequent unexplained changes that create confusion or force teams to relearn expectations too often. Communicate each update with the reason for the change and the practical impact on day-to-day development work.

Sustain the capability by treating the AI stance as a living source of guidance rather than a one-time policy artifact. Review it regularly against new tools, developer questions, and changes in AI usage patterns while keeping changes stable enough for teams to absorb. Continue measuring comprehension through surveys, policy engagement, and clarification volume. Watch for regression signals such as shadow AI use, repeated approval questions, or uncertainty about whether the stance applies to specific roles.

name: Code maintainability
question: Team members can easily find and reuse relevant code examples, propose changes across team-owned code when needed, and add, update, trace, and test dependencies without frequent breakage or unclear ownership.
description: Code maintainability means that project teams can work effectively within a growing codebase without being blocked by unclear ownership, poor discoverability, or fragile dependencies. In practice, team members can search the relevant codebase, find examples, reuse existing code, and propose changes to code maintained by other teams through controlled review mechanisms. The capability also covers dependency management, including adding dependencies, upgrading them, knowing which versions are used, and detecting breaking changes through automated testing. Observable practices include accessible source control, searchable code, review and approval paths for cross-team changes, reproducible builds, and traceable dependency versions. The intent is to make routine changes, refactoring, emergency fixes, and vulnerability patching faster and safer across the software portfolio.

guidance.text: Start by identifying where code and dependency information is fragmented or inaccessible. Make the most relevant repositories searchable for the team and document how developers can find examples, owners, and existing implementations. Establish a basic review path for proposing changes to code outside the team’s direct write access. Create an initial inventory of dependencies and the applications that use them.

Strengthen source code access by reducing unnecessary repository restrictions and standardizing where developers search for code. Introduce or clarify pull request workflows so cross-team changes can be proposed, reviewed, approved, and audited without bypassing controls. Define conventions for dependency declarations so versions are visible in version control. Begin checking whether dependency updates can be built and tested consistently across developer machines and CI.

Improve traceability by ensuring each build or deployment can be linked to the exact versions of all dependencies used. Use automated CI and tests to detect whether dependency upgrades or shared code changes break affected systems. Measure how long it takes to make changes in code owned by another team and where reviews or approvals slow the process. Remove duplicated or unused code when it is discovered during normal maintenance work.

Expand maintainability practices across more of the organization’s codebase rather than relying on team-specific habits. Standardize dependency update processes so teams can consume known-good versions and respond quickly when vulnerabilities are patched. Strengthen tooling that shows which services are affected by a dependency or code change before it reaches production. Track metrics such as searchable code coverage, dependency version age, upgrade frequency, and lead time for cross-team changes.

Sustain performance by regularly testing whether emergency changes and vulnerability patches can move through the normal review, test, and release path quickly. Keep dependency versions consistent where possible and continue measuring whether older or unsupported versions are accumulating. Review access, search, ownership, and approval mechanisms periodically so growth in repositories or teams does not recreate hidden barriers. Protect reliability by avoiding shortcut processes that skip validation in favor of speed.

name: Continuous delivery
question: To what extent do you agree that software changes remain deployable throughout development because the team fixes build, test, security, data, and deployment issues quickly, uses automated feedback, and can release safely on demand during normal business hours?
description: Continuous delivery means a software project keeps changes releasable throughout the development lifecycle and can release them to users whenever needed with low risk. In practice, it is visible when builds, tests, security checks, deployment steps, configuration, database changes, and production artifacts are handled as parts of one delivery system rather than as late-stage activities. Teams demonstrating this capability use fast feedback to detect whether a change is still deployable and treat broken builds, failing tests, or release blockers as urgent signals. Deployments are routine enough to happen during normal business hours without special disruption to users or staff. The capability applies across software contexts such as services, infrastructure, databases, firmware, mobile apps, and regulated systems.

guidance.text: Start by making the current release path visible: map how a typical change moves from version control to production, including testing, security review, change management, and release steps. Put application code, configuration, environment scripts, and database change scripts under version control if any are still handled outside it. Identify manual deployment activities and late feedback points that make releases unsafe or unpredictable. Choose one high-impact bottleneck and create a small automated check or deployment step that gives the team earlier evidence that a change can be released.

Strengthen continuous integration so every check-in creates a canonical build and runs a fast set of automated tests. Treat failing builds, failing tests, and broken deployment scripts as work that interrupts feature development until the system is releasable again. Shorten branch lifetimes by merging small changes to the mainline frequently and avoiding code freezes or stabilization periods. Begin automating the deployment path to test environments so release readiness does not depend on manual coordination.

Expand testing from a late phase into normal development work by having developers and testers review, maintain, and improve automated test suites together. Ensure test data can be obtained on demand and does not limit how often important tests run. Add security reviews, approved libraries, and security checks to design discussions and automated testing rather than treating security as a final gate. Track elapsed time, value-add time, and rework for release-related steps so the team can see which processes delay deployability.

Focus on making production release a routine daytime activity by reducing remaining manual approvals, handoffs, and environment-specific deployment differences. Strengthen monitoring, observability, and proactive notifications so the team can understand user-visible system health and respond quickly when a release causes degradation. Review architecture and ownership boundaries where releases still require orchestration with other services, and reduce coupling that prevents independent deployment. Measure lead time, change failure rate, restoration time, release frequency, rework, and deployment pain to verify that delivery changes are lowering risk.

Keep the system in a releasable state by protecting fast feedback, reliable tests, version-controlled production artifacts, and automated deployments from gradual erosion. Continue value stream mapping periodically, especially when new manual controls, technical debt, or process layers begin to slow changes. Maintain collaboration across development, testing, security, operations, and other delivery participants so process and toolchain changes remain aligned. Use delivery and reliability outcomes to decide where to invest improvement capacity, and avoid increasing release frequency unless the supporting technical practices remain healthy.

name: Continuous integration
question: Software changes are integrated into the main branch at least daily, with each commit automatically triggering a repeatable build, fast automated tests, visible status, and immediate action when a build or test failure occurs.
description: Continuous integration means developers frequently merge small code changes into the shared mainline and validate them with automated builds and tests. Each commit is expected to trigger a repeatable build and fast automated test feedback, so integration problems are found soon after they are introduced. The team keeps the software in a working state by making build and test status visible and by treating broken builds as urgent problems to fix or revert. It also relies on short-lived branches, daily integration, and tests that run quickly enough for developers to use them repeatedly. In practice, this capability is visible when builds are automated, tests run on each change, feedback arrives quickly, and testers can access current working builds.

guidance.text: Start by ensuring that everything needed to build and configure the application is stored in the code repository. Create an automated build script that produces repeatable, numbered packages deployable to any environment. Remove manual build steps that create undocumented variation or opportunities for mistakes. Establish a daily target for running the automated build successfully.

Connect commits to the CI system so that each check-in triggers the automated build without manual intervention. Add a small set of reliable unit and acceptance tests that cover high-value system behavior. Keep these tests fast enough to provide feedback within a few minutes, and treat unreliable tests as problems to fix rather than signals to ignore. Begin covering new functionality with automated tests as it is added.

Strengthen integration habits by merging work into trunk or mainline at least daily instead of relying on long-lived feature branches. Make build and test status visible in a shared team channel or display so failures are noticed immediately. When the build breaks, pause other work long enough to identify and fix the issue. If the cause cannot be fixed quickly, revert the breaking change to restore a working mainline.

Expand feedback beyond the fastest tests by ensuring developers receive daily results from acceptance and performance tests where those tests exist. Keep current builds available to testers for exploratory testing so feedback is based on recent integrated code. Measure the percentage of commits that trigger builds and automated tests without manual intervention. Track the time from a broken build to a fix or revert, and use that data to shorten feedback loops.

Sustain the practice by keeping builds repeatable, test suites reliable, and fast feedback available on every change. Watch for regression toward long-lived branches, ignored notifications, slow tests, or broken builds that remain unresolved. Regularly review CI measures such as successful daily builds, successful daily test runs, tester build availability, and build repair time. Keep immediate fix-or-revert behavior explicit so the mainline remains a stable base for ongoing development.

name: Customer feedback
question: The software project team regularly gathers user feedback and product usage metrics early enough to shape feature decisions, validate real customer problems, adjust designs, and confirm whether delivered changes improve intended customer outcomes.
description: Customer feedback means using direct input from users and evidence from product use to understand whether software solves real problems. In practice, teams collect satisfaction signals, usage metrics, and qualitative feedback before and after feature decisions are made. This capability is visible when teams validate problems before building, test possible solutions with real users, and adjust features based on what they learn. It also means success is judged by customer and business outcomes rather than only by whether a specified feature was delivered. The capability applies to both external products and internal tools, because both require frequent engagement with real users to avoid building software that is not useful.

guidance.text: Start by identifying where customer or user feedback is currently missing from the delivery process. Add at least one lightweight feedback activity before feature implementation begins, such as user interviews, support-ticket review, or prototype testing. Stop treating finalized requirements as sufficient proof that the problem is understood. Track whether each significant feature has a clearly stated customer problem before design or coding starts.

Strengthen feedback collection by making it regular rather than occasional. Define a small set of customer satisfaction or product usage measures that are visible to the team and updated on a predictable cadence. Use feedback early enough that it can still change the feature scope, design, or priority. Measure how often features are changed or stopped because user research shows a different problem or need.

Make feedback part of the normal delivery workflow for significant changes. Validate proposed solutions with users through prototypes, experiments, or comparable research before committing to full production implementation. Strengthen the link between feedback and decisions by recording what was learned and what changed as a result. Stop measuring success only by delivery completion, and include whether the feature addressed the intended customer outcome.

Increase the precision of feedback-driven decisions by connecting qualitative user input with measurable product outcomes. Use metrics such as adoption, retention, referral, revenue, or product-specific equivalents where they fit the context. Strengthen experimentation practices so the team can compare alternatives and learn which solutions improve outcomes. Measure how often delivered changes improve, fail to move, or negatively affect the selected customer or business metrics.

Sustain the capability by keeping customer feedback visible, current, and actively used in product decisions. Continue validating significant features before building them and revisiting results after release to confirm whether the expected outcome occurred. Protect the team’s ability to change designs or reduce scope when feedback shows that a proposed solution does not solve the problem. Watch for regression signals such as feedback arriving too late, metrics becoming stale, or success being defined only as delivering the requested feature.

name: Database change management
question: Database changes are discussed with database owners, stored as version-controlled migration scripts, tracked across environments, tested with production-like data, and applied through automated processes without slowing or destabilizing application deployments.
description: Database change management means handling database updates as an integrated part of normal software delivery rather than as a separate, risky release activity. Teams make database changes visible by discussing upcoming work with the people responsible for production databases and tracking what has been applied in each environment. Schema changes are captured as ordered migration scripts in version control and managed alongside application code. Automated tooling applies pending migrations and records the result, creating a shared source of truth for deployment status. In practice, the capability is visible when database work does not become a hidden dependency that delays releases or creates deployment failures.

guidance.text: Start by identifying where database changes currently originate, who approves them, and how they are applied in each environment. Stop relying on undocumented manual edits or separate side processes that are invisible to the software delivery team. Create a basic record of pending and completed database changes so developers, DBAs, and release participants can see the same information. Use recent deployment delays or failures to identify where database work is creating the most friction.

Move recurring database changes into version control as ordered scripts stored with the related application code. Strengthen communication by reviewing application changes that require database updates with the people responsible for production databases before deployment planning is finalized. Begin tracking which scripts have been applied to development, test, staging, and production environments. Measure how often deployment preparation is delayed because database change status is unclear.

Standardize the use of migration scripts so each database schema change has a clear sequence, ownership, and execution record. Use tooling that records which migrations have run against each database instance and whether they succeeded. Test migrations before release using data and database topology that resemble production closely enough to reveal performance or distributed-system issues. Track failed deployments where database changes contributed, and use those findings to refine the migration process.

Strengthen automation so database migrations can be applied through the same controlled delivery process used for application changes. For changes that could cause downtime, apply patterns such as online schema migration or parallel change to separate schema updates from application rollout. Ensure rollback and compatibility concerns are considered when new and old database structures coexist during deployment. Measure the share of database changes completed through the automated process and the lead-time impact of database work.

Sustain the capability by keeping all database schema changes version-controlled, migration-based, visible, and applied through automated tooling. Continue testing each schema change against production-like data and infrastructure so performance or availability risks are caught before release. Review deployment incidents and lead-time data to confirm that database work is not reintroducing delays, failures, or scheduled downtime. Guard against regression when new applications share schemas, new teams join, or architectural changes alter how data is partitioned, archived, or migrated.

name: Deployment automation
question: The team's deployment process can deploy the same versioned build to test or production environments on demand, using automated scripts and configuration from version control, with minimal manual steps and clear deployment test results.
description: Deployment automation means that software can be moved into testing or production through a repeatable automated process rather than a sequence of manual actions. In practice, teams use versioned build artifacts, deployment scripts, environment configuration, and automated checks to make deployments consistent across environments. The same process and packages are used for testing and production so that what is released has already been exercised in earlier environments. The deployment process records what was deployed, where it was deployed, and what happened during deployment so that results can be reviewed. Teams demonstrating this capability can deploy on demand with limited waiting, fewer handoffs, and less room for environment-specific variation.

guidance.text: Start by documenting the current deployment process in a shared place that both development and operations roles can access. Identify every manual action, handoff, approval wait, environment preparation step, configuration change, and verification step. Separate build packaging, environment configuration, deployment execution, database changes, and smoke testing into visible parts. Use this map to choose the first repeatable manual step that can be scripted without changing the whole process at once.

Strengthen automation by turning the most frequent and error-prone deployment actions into scripts that can be run consistently. Store deployment scripts and environment configuration in version control so changes can be reviewed and reproduced. Use build artifacts produced by continuous integration instead of rebuilding separately for each environment. Add a basic deployment test that confirms the application starts and required external services are reachable.

Make the deployment process consistent across environments by using the same deployment mechanism for testing, staging, and production. Keep environment-specific configuration separate from the deployable package so the same artifact can move through the pipeline. Reduce ticket-based handoffs by allowing authorized team members to deploy approved artifact versions on demand. Track how many manual steps remain and measure where deployment work waits between pipeline stages.

Improve reliability by making deployment steps idempotent so failed or interrupted steps can be safely repeated. Reduce order dependencies by changing services and components so they tolerate missing dependencies or operate in a controlled degraded mode. Automate database migrations and configuration generation as part of the same repeatable process rather than treating them as separate manual tasks. Ensure deployment tooling records which build is in each environment and preserves deployment output for later review.

Sustain the capability by keeping the deployment process simple, repeatable, and shared between development and operations roles. Review new services, infrastructure changes, and tools for whether they can be deployed through the existing automated process without console-based manual work. Continue measuring manual steps, automation coverage, and pipeline delays to detect regression early. Preserve the ability to recreate any environment from version-controlled information and artifact repository contents.

name: Documentation quality
question: Team members can find, trust, and use current internal documentation that clearly explains relevant systems, decisions, and technical practices, and the team actively creates and maintains documentation as part of normal development work.
description: Documentation quality means that internal documentation is treated as part of the software development work, not as a separate or optional artifact. It covers whether documentation is clear enough to understand, easy enough to locate, and reliable enough for teams to use when making technical decisions. In practice, this capability is visible when teams create and update documentation as systems, practices, and decisions change. It also means that documentation supports the adoption and operation of technical practices across the software delivery process. The capability focuses on documentation as a usable, maintained resource that helps teams work consistently and effectively.

guidance.text: Start by identifying the documentation that team members most often need but cannot easily find or trust. Create a short inventory of missing, outdated, or unclear pages related to current systems and technical practices. Assign ownership for the highest-priority items so that documentation work is not left to chance. Measure progress by tracking whether those critical documents now exist and are discoverable.

Strengthen the documentation that already exists by checking it for clarity, findability, and reliability. Remove or mark outdated pages so team members do not rely on conflicting information. Add basic context to important documents, such as what the document covers, when it was last updated, and who can answer questions. Track whether team members can locate the right documentation without needing repeated direct help.

Make documentation creation and maintenance part of normal development work when systems, decisions, or technical practices change. Add documentation checks to regular workflows such as design discussions, implementation tasks, or release preparation. Encourage contributors to update relevant pages at the same time they change the software or process being described. Measure whether changes that affect team knowledge consistently result in updated documentation.

Improve consistency by using shared documentation guidelines, templates, or review expectations where they help make documents easier to read and compare. Work with people who have documentation expertise or act as documentation champions when important technical content needs refinement. Review whether documentation supports the technical practices the team depends on, such as delivery, integration, reliability, security, or version control practices. Measure whether team members report that documentation is clear, findable, and dependable across these areas.

Sustain documentation quality by keeping ownership, review, and maintenance responsibilities visible over time. Continue checking that documentation remains current as systems and technical practices evolve. Watch for regression signals such as stale pages, repeated questions, or team members bypassing documentation because they do not trust it. Preserve performance by treating documentation work as ongoing technical work with measurable usefulness, not as a one-time cleanup activity.

name: Empowering teams to choose tools
question: Team members can select tools and technologies for development, testing, deployment, monitoring, or data work based on project needs, while documenting exceptions, considering support costs, and participating in regular reviews of the team’s toolset.
description: This capability means that software project teams have meaningful input into the tools and technologies they use for their work. In practice, teams choose tools based on the tasks they need to perform, while considering maintainability, support effort, technical debt, and coordination with other teams. The capability is visible when teams use an agreed baseline toolset, review whether current tools still meet project needs, and discuss new technologies in a structured way. It also includes having a clear process for using tools outside the baseline, including documenting what was chosen and why. Teams remain responsible for supporting the technologies they introduce, rather than treating tool choice as separate from long-term delivery and operations.

guidance.text: Start by identifying where tool decisions are currently made and whether project team members have any practical way to influence them. Create a simple inventory of the tools used for development, testing, deployment, monitoring, and data work. Ask team members which tools block or slow their work, and capture concrete examples tied to project tasks. Use those examples to open a focused discussion with decision makers about where team input is needed before tool choices are finalized.

Establish a visible baseline of tools that are approved for common project needs, such as programming languages, libraries, test tools, deployment tools, monitoring systems, and data backends. Include representatives from relevant roles when defining or updating that baseline, including developers, testers, operators, and product roles where appropriate. Stop treating the baseline as a fixed list that cannot be questioned. Measure whether team members know which tools are available and where to raise concerns about gaps.

Strengthen the review process by evaluating the current toolset during retrospectives or other recurring team forums. Focus each review on how well tools support current requirements, where they create friction, and whether a new tool should be explored. Define a lightweight exception process for using tools outside the baseline, including who reviews the decision and what must be documented. Track exceptions so the team can later determine whether a tool should remain project-specific or be considered for the baseline.

Make tool experiments more deliberate by testing proposed technologies against real project needs before adopting them. Compare existing and proposed tools on observable criteria such as implementation effort, licensing, support needs, infrastructure requirements, operational complexity, and maintainability. Document the expected benefits and the responsibilities the team accepts when introducing a new technology. Share findings through team presentations or cross-team discussions so others can understand costs, risks, and useful patterns.

Sustain the capability by keeping tool choice connected to delivery outcomes, support responsibilities, and cross-team coordination. Continue periodic reviews even when the current toolset appears stable, because sticking with existing tools can be the right decision when it is intentional. Watch for regression toward either extreme: forcing one toolset in all cases or allowing unmanaged tool proliferation across projects. Maintain clear documentation for baseline tools, exceptions, experiments, and support ownership so future teams can understand why choices were made.

name: Flexible infrastructure
question: Team members can access, provision, change, scale, and monitor shared infrastructure through automated self-service mechanisms, with usage and costs measured transparently, without manual tickets, long waits, or undocumented configuration changes.
description: Flexible infrastructure means that software teams can obtain and adjust computing resources when they need them, without depending on slow manual handoffs. In practice, this includes self-service provisioning, network-accessible resources, pooled capacity, elastic scaling, and measured usage. Teams demonstrate this capability when infrastructure changes are automated, traceable, and available through defined mechanisms rather than ad hoc requests. It also means that infrastructure use can be observed and connected to costs, products, teams, or environments. The practical intent is to make infrastructure support faster, more reliable delivery while preserving control, auditability, and cost transparency.

guidance.text: Start by identifying the infrastructure requests that currently require tickets, emails, or manual approval before work can continue. Measure how long it takes to obtain test environments, provision new resources, and complete configuration changes. Stop treating cloud-hosted resources as flexible if the team still depends on traditional datacenter-style processes. Select one frequent request type and define what an automated, traceable self-service path should produce.

Strengthen the emerging self-service process by moving infrastructure configuration into version control. Define which changes can be requested by team members through an automated mechanism and which require review by another role or group. Start logging provisioning actions, deployment actions, and configuration outputs so changes can be audited. Track whether the new path reduces waiting time compared with the older ticket-based process.

Expand automation beyond a narrow use case so teams can provision environments, make approved configuration changes, and execute deployments through the same controlled mechanism. Use infrastructure as code to make changes repeatable rather than dependent on individual operators. Strengthen collaboration between development, operations, security, finance, and procurement so controls, cost tracking, and delivery needs are not handled separately. Measure end-to-end infrastructure change time and the proportion of resources managed through automated patterns.

Improve elasticity and cost visibility by ensuring resources can scale outward or inward on demand and that usage is measured by product, team, environment, or business line. Review whether pooled infrastructure, logical separation between environments, and chargeback or similar mechanisms are working as intended. Strengthen governance so self-service does not create cloud sprawl, orphaned infrastructure, or unreviewed configuration drift. Track expensive applications, unused resources, time-to-live for hosts, and how often infrastructure changes are completed without manual intervention.

Sustain performance by regularly checking that the team still meets the five cloud characteristics in day-to-day practice, not only in platform design. Keep infrastructure configuration, approvals, automation logs, and cost reporting visible enough to support auditability and informed decisions. Watch for regression into manual tickets, long-lived unpatched hosts, undocumented changes, or fixed datacenter-style processes. Continue scaling effective cloud-native patterns while measuring whether infrastructure practices support rapid, reliable releases, high availability, and transparent operating costs.

name: Generative organizational culture
question: How strongly do you agree that team members actively seek and share timely usable information, raise bad news without punishment, share responsibility for risks, collaborate across functions, examine failures without blame, and try new ideas in daily software delivery work?
description: Generative organizational culture describes a high-trust environment where information moves quickly to the people who need it and is useful for action. In practice, software teams show this through strong cooperation, shared responsibility for delivery and operations, and active collaboration across functional boundaries. Team members can raise failures, risks, and bad news without fear of blame or punishment. Problems are examined to understand system and process causes, not to single out individuals. New ideas and experiments are welcomed when they can improve products, processes, or collaboration.

guidance.text: Start by making information flow visible in daily work. Add a regular team habit for asking what information is missing, who needs it, and whether it is timely enough to act on. Create a simple agreement that bad news, risks, and failures should be raised early and discussed without blame. Select one recent incident or delivery problem and review it for process and system causes rather than individual fault.

Strengthen cooperation by involving the functions needed to build, deploy, secure, and operate the product in planning and delivery discussions. Identify where responsibilities are currently narrow or unclear, especially for quality, reliability, availability, and security. Choose one shared responsibility area and define how developers, operations, security, and quality roles will contribute together. Track whether risks are surfaced earlier and whether more people can explain the delivery and operational impacts.

Focus on breaking down silos that still slow information flow. Include operational and other downstream perspectives earlier in the software delivery lifecycle, not only after implementation decisions are made. Use practical bridging mechanisms such as shared planning sessions, direct cross-functional conversations, or lightweight shared communication channels for delivery issues. Measure whether questions are reaching the right people faster and whether unresolved handoffs are decreasing.

Strengthen the response to failure and novelty so they consistently lead to learning and useful change. Run blameless postmortems for significant incidents and convert findings into specific improvements to tools, processes, or technical systems. Create protected opportunities for experimentation, such as small trials, internal sharing sessions, or improvements to repetitive work. Review whether experiments and postmortem actions are actually implemented rather than only discussed.

Sustain performance by regularly checking that the six core behaviors remain visible: cooperation, safe reporting, shared risks, bridging, inquiry after failure, and implemented novelty. Continue measuring team perceptions with survey items about information seeking, shared responsibilities, cross-functional collaboration, treatment of failures, and openness to new ideas. Watch for regression signals such as ignored bad news, blame after incidents, local silos, or repeated use of “the way it has always been done.” Keep reinforcing practices that make information timely, usable, and available to the people who need it.

name: Healthy data ecosystems
question: Team members can reliably find, access, understand, and use the internal data needed for development work, because key data sources are well documented, consistently maintained, quality-checked, and not trapped in isolated tools or silos.
description: Healthy data ecosystems mean that software project teams can depend on internal data as a usable part of everyday engineering work, rather than treating it as scattered output from systems. The capability is visible when important data sources are discoverable, understandable, accurate enough for decisions, and available through secure paths. It also means that critical data domains have clear ownership, supporting metadata, and practices that keep data current and consistent across services and tools. In practice, teams spend less time searching across disconnected sources and more time applying trusted context in development, analysis, and AI-assisted workflows. This capability gives developers and AI tools organization-specific context without relying on fragmented or poorly maintained data.

guidance.text: Start by identifying the internal data sources most often needed for one important service, workflow, or application. Assign a clear owner or steward for each source so that accuracy, access rules, and basic metadata are no longer unmanaged. Stop relying on informal knowledge or ad hoc requests as the primary way to locate data. Measure how long it takes team members to find and gain access to the data needed for common development tasks.

Strengthen the initial data inventory by documenting what each selected data source contains, where it is created, how it is consumed, and who maintains it. Move documentation close to the related application or data artifact, such as a repository README or versioned metadata file. Start separating reliable sources from duplicated, outdated, or unclear copies. Track repeated questions, access delays, and cases where team members cannot determine which source is authoritative.

Create a more consistent view of key data by consolidating or federating sources that describe the same domain. Add automated checks for accuracy, completeness, timeliness, and freshness on the datasets most relied on by the team. Strengthen secure access paths so team members and approved tools can discover and use data without bypassing governance. Measure data-related defects, incidents, and cases where missing context slows development or analysis.

Extend ownership, documentation, quality checks, and governed access beyond the initial pilot area to other high-value services or domains. Treat data changes like other engineering changes by reviewing metadata, validation rules, and downstream consumers when schemas or sources change. Stop allowing individual tools to define isolated data architectures when shared integration is required. Measure whether developers and AI-enabled workflows can consistently reach definitive answers from trusted internal data within an expected time.

Sustain performance by regularly reviewing ownership, metadata, access paths, and automated data checks for key domains. Keep documentation versioned and close to the data so it changes together with the systems that create or consume it. Watch for regression signals such as growing access delays, stale datasets, duplicated sources, or incidents caused by poor data quality. Continue validating that internal data remains usable for both human development work and AI-assisted workflows without weakening governance.

name: Job satisfaction
question: Team members have the tools, technical resources, learning support, meaningful work, and opportunity to apply professional judgment needed to do effective software delivery work in day-to-day project activities without unnecessary barriers or underuse of their expertise.
description: Job satisfaction means that software project team members experience their work as meaningful, challenging, and supported by the organization. In practice, this is visible when people have the tools, environments, learning resources, and other support needed to complete their work effectively. It also means that team members can use their skills and judgment rather than being blocked by missing resources or decisions made far from the work. The capability connects day-to-day work conditions with engagement, creativity, and stronger thinking in software delivery. It is assessed through people’s perceptions because job satisfaction cannot be reliably inferred from system data alone.

guidance.text: Start by identifying the most immediate blockers that prevent team members from doing their work, such as missing tools, unavailable test environments, slow access to technical resources, or unclear ownership of decisions. Create a short, visible list of these obstacles and assign owners to remove them. Ask team members which work feels disconnected from their expertise or has little visible value, then use that input to adjust task allocation. Measure progress by tracking whether the most common resource and meaning-related blockers are resolved within agreed timeframes.

Strengthen the basics by making tool and resource needs part of regular planning rather than treating them as exceptions. Review whether team members have access to the environments, systems, documentation, training materials, and budgets needed for their current work. Stop assigning improvement work without time or resources to carry it out. Measure whether blocked work caused by missing resources decreases across iterations.

Build a more consistent practice for matching work to expertise and giving practitioners influence over the tools they use. Include team input when selecting or changing tools, especially where those tools affect development, testing, integration, or delivery workflows. Make meaningful work more visible by connecting backlog items and improvement tasks to user, product, operational, or team outcomes. Measure whether team members report that their judgment is considered in technical decisions that affect their daily work.

Strengthen the feedback loop between support, meaningful work, and delivery performance. Regularly review whether current tools, environments, and learning resources still fit how the team actually works. Protect time for process improvement and skill development, especially when adopting practices such as automation or continuous integration. Measure both perceived job satisfaction and observable follow-through, such as completion of improvement work and removal of recurring delivery obstacles.

Sustain performance by keeping job satisfaction visible as a team and organizational signal rather than assuming current conditions will remain healthy. Continue asking team members directly about meaningful work, resource needs, tool effectiveness, and whether their judgment is valued. Watch for regression during technology changes, reorganizations, or major process shifts, when people may need extra time, learning support, and clearer decision rights. Maintain regular measurement through perception-based feedback and act on trends before they affect engagement or delivery outcomes.

name: Learning culture
question: Learning opportunities are treated as normal work: team members have time and resources to explore ideas, share knowledge regularly, learn from failures without blame, and bring insights from training or conferences into team practice.
description: Learning culture describes whether software teams treat learning as a practical part of delivering and improving software, rather than as an occasional side activity. In day-to-day work, it is visible when people have time, budget, and permission to build skills, investigate ideas, and learn informally. It also appears when teams discuss failures without blame and use them to understand how processes or systems can work better. The capability includes regular knowledge sharing, such as talks, trip reports, study groups, or other recurring ways to spread new ideas across the team. For respondents, the survey question asks whether learning is actively supported through observable routines, resources, and team behaviors.

guidance.text: Start by making learning visible as a legitimate team activity rather than something done only after hours. Identify one small, recurring practice such as a monthly knowledge-sharing session or short discussion of lessons from recent work. Stop treating failures only as individual mistakes, and begin recording what the team can learn about processes, tools, or system behavior. Measure whether team members can name at least one protected opportunity to learn during normal work.

Strengthen the basic routines by assigning time and ownership for learning activities. Create a simple cadence for informal learning, such as lightning talks, lunch-and-learns, or shared exploration of new tools. Begin using blameless post-incident or post-release discussions to turn problems into concrete learning points. Track participation and whether learning outcomes lead to shared notes, process changes, or follow-up experiments.

Expand learning beyond isolated events by connecting it to team planning and delivery work. Allocate explicit time or budget for training, certifications, conferences, or structured study groups where relevant to the team’s work. Ask people who attend external learning events to share useful ideas through presentations or short trip reports. Measure whether learning inputs are regularly converted into team practices, experiments, or documented improvements.

Make learning practices more reliable and distributed across the team. Rotate presenters, facilitators, and study-group leads so knowledge sharing is not dependent on a few people. Strengthen psychological safety by reviewing failures for process and system lessons, not blame, and by encouraging reasonable technical exploration. Measure whether team members consistently report having access to time, resources, and safe spaces for learning.

Sustain the learning culture by keeping resources, cadence, and psychological safety visible even when delivery pressure increases. Continue funding formal and informal learning, and review whether the investment is reaching different roles across the software team. Keep knowledge-sharing routines fresh by rotating formats, topics, and contributors while maintaining a predictable schedule. Watch for regression signals such as canceled learning time, low sharing participation, or failure reviews that shift back toward blame.

name: Loosely coupled teams
question: Software project teams can change, test, deploy, and release their services independently during normal business hours, with minimal cross-team coordination, without shared integrated test environments, and with negligible downtime.
description: Loosely coupled teams can deliver changes without relying heavily on other teams for decisions, testing, deployment, or release timing. In practice, this means a team’s service or component has clear boundaries, stable interfaces, and enough autonomy to make design changes without external approval for routine work. Teams can validate most changes on demand, often by testing their service in isolation rather than waiting for a shared integrated environment. Deployments and releases can happen independently during normal business hours with little disruption to users or dependent services. The capability is visible when work moves through development, validation, and production with few handoffs, limited waiting, and low coordination overhead.

guidance.text: Start by identifying where the team is blocked by outside approvals, shared environments, coordinated releases, or dependencies on other teams. Map a recent change from code complete to release and record each handoff, wait time, and required coordination point. Stop treating these delays as unavoidable and make the main sources of coupling visible to the team and stakeholders. Select one high-friction dependency to reduce first, such as a manual approval step, unavailable test environment, or release dependency.

Strengthen the team’s ability to complete small changes without fine-grained coordination outside the team. Define clearer ownership boundaries for the service or component, including which decisions the team can make independently and which still require escalation. Begin replacing shared validation bottlenecks with on-demand testing options, such as mocks, stubs, fakes, or service virtualization for external dependencies. Measure how many changes still require external approval, coordinated deployment, or access to a scarce integrated test environment.

Focus on making independent testing and deployment reliable for common changes. Expand contract tests and clear service interfaces so dependencies can be checked without requiring every service to be deployed together. Reduce release coupling by separating deployment from release where possible and by ensuring dependent services can tolerate compatible changes. Track deployment dependencies, functional validation completed outside shared environments, and time spent coordinating with other teams.

Strengthen architectural and operational practices that support deploy-anytime behavior. Use backward-compatible APIs, versioning discipline, and deployment approaches such as rolling or blue/green deployments to reduce downtime and coordination needs. Monitor whether upstream service changes or failures still force unplanned work or service degradation, then address the highest-impact coupling points. Keep releases small and independent, and avoid returning to large coordinated deployments when deadlines or incidents create pressure.

Sustain performance by continuously checking that team autonomy, service boundaries, testing independence, and deployment independence remain intact as the product evolves. Review metrics such as coordinated deployment percentage, external approval frequency, contract test failures, downtime during deployment, and cross-team coordination time. Retire unnecessary API versions and outdated dependencies before they create maintenance drag or renewed coupling. When adding new functionality, preserve bounded service responsibilities and verify that the team can still build, test, deploy, and release safely without broad coordination.

name: Monitoring and observability
question: Team members can use monitoring and observability data, including metrics, logs, traces, alerts, and dashboards, to detect user-facing problems, understand production behavior, and diagnose issues across relevant services and infrastructure.
description: Monitoring and observability means that a software project team can see how production systems are behaving and investigate problems using reliable operational data. In practice, this includes health reporting, customer-experience signals, key business and system metrics, and tools for debugging production behavior. The capability covers both predefined monitoring data, such as metrics and logs, and exploratory analysis that helps uncover patterns or failures that were not known in advance. Team members have access to the data and tools needed to trace issues across applications, services, infrastructure, and customer-facing interactions. The practical focus is on detecting outages, degradations, bugs, unauthorized activity, and unexpected side effects of changes, then using that information to understand what happened.

guidance.text: Start by identifying the most important production signals that show whether the system is functioning and whether customers are affected. Add basic health checks, resource metrics, and logs for critical services before expanding into more detailed instrumentation. Stop relying only on manual reports or customer complaints to discover failures. Measure whether outages or degradations are detected before users report them.

Strengthen monitoring so alerts are tied to visible service symptoms rather than every possible internal cause. Add instrumentation for key user journeys, important system dependencies, and business-critical workflows. Make sure developers can access monitoring data directly instead of depending on one specialist or operations group. Track false negatives, especially incidents where alerts were missing or arrived too late.

Expand observability by connecting metrics, structured logs, and traces across applications, infrastructure, and dependent services. Improve dashboards and alert links so engineers can move quickly from an alert to relevant context, runbooks, and diagnostic views. Remove alerts that are not actionable or repeatedly produce no improvement. Measure false positives, alert acknowledgement time, and whether engineers can understand alerts without extensive handoff.

Treat monitoring configuration as shared, reviewable work by keeping changes under version control and applying them through an automated deployment process. Use postmortems and retrospectives to update instrumentation, dashboards, and symptom-based alerts after outages or unexpected side effects. Make it easy for team members to create fit-for-purpose visualizations instead of depending on a fixed perfect dashboard. Measure changes to monitoring configuration, alert silencing, unactionable alerts, MTTD, MTTR, and customer impact.

Sustain performance by regularly reviewing whether monitoring and observability still match the current system architecture, customer journeys, and operational risks. Keep alert noise low by deleting or refining unactionable alerts and carefully tracking silences or suppressions. Continue sharing responsibility for instrumentation and monitoring changes across developers, rather than allowing ownership to concentrate in one role. Watch leading indicators such as night-time alerts, alert balance, acknowledgement time, MTTD, MTTR, and customer impact to prevent regression.

name: Monitoring systems to inform business decisions
question: The team collects monitoring data from application performance, infrastructure, testing, and delivery activities, shares it in understandable forms, and uses contextualized data in team and business discussions to guide decisions before problems become critical.
description: This capability means that software project teams collect and use information about applications, infrastructure, and delivery activities to understand how systems and work are performing. Monitoring data gives teams timely feedback about system behavior, emerging issues, deployment outcomes, and customer usage patterns. In practice, teams make this information visible across development, operations, product, and business stakeholders so that decisions are based on relevant evidence rather than isolated observations. The data is transformed, visualized, and explained in ways that different audiences can understand and apply during reports, briefings, and meetings. The capability is demonstrated when monitoring information is available, shared with context, and actively used to guide technical and business decisions.

guidance.text: Start by identifying which application, infrastructure, testing, QA, and operations data would give the team basic visibility into current system and delivery behavior. Stop relying only on incident reports, outages, or ad hoc observations as the main source of system knowledge. Choose a small set of functionally relevant metrics and make them available in a shared place, even if the first format is simple. Review whether the selected data can answer practical questions about system health, deployment outcomes, and customer usage.

Strengthen monitoring coverage beyond the most visible components by checking for gaps across the software development and delivery pipeline. Add data sources that show work before production, such as testing, QA, deployment, and operational handoff signals. Start using thresholds or trend views that reveal approaching problems rather than waiting for systems to fail. Measure whether the team can detect important changes early enough to discuss and act on them.

Make collected monitoring data easier to use by transforming and visualizing it for the audiences that need it. Add context to dashboards, reports, or meeting materials so people can tell whether values are expected, unusual, changing, or different from historical patterns. Share operations knowledge upstream with development and product stakeholders when it can inform design or prioritization decisions. Track whether monitoring data is actually referenced in technical and business discussions, not only collected.

Strengthen decision use by bringing relevant, timely, accurate, and understandable monitoring data into regular planning, delivery, and business review meetings. Stop presenting raw numbers without explaining how technology, infrastructure, deployments, or customer usage may have influenced them. Compare local improvements with broader system impact so decisions do not optimize one service while missing wider infrastructure opportunities. Measure whether decisions, follow-up actions, and learning from incidents or deployments are connected back to monitoring evidence.

Sustain performance by regularly reviewing whether the monitored areas still reflect the most important parts of the value chain and business context. Avoid regression by pruning noisy, low-value alerts and reports that distract from key signals or create data overload. Keep matching presentation formats to usage, such as real-time dashboards for operational decisions and periodic reports for longer-term business discussions. Continue checking that monitoring information remains available, shared, contextualized, and used to guide decisions across technical and business stakeholders.

name: Pervasive security
question: Software project team members involve InfoSec early in design, use approved libraries and tools, include security requirements in automated tests, and address security feedback throughout development, testing, demos, and release activities.
description: Pervasive security means treating security as a normal part of software delivery rather than a late-stage inspection activity. In practice, development, QA, operations, and InfoSec collaborate across design, development, testing, and release work. Teams make security concerns visible early, including during feature design and application demos, so weaknesses can be found before they become expensive to change. Security expectations are reflected in approved tools, libraries, packages, and processes that developers can use in daily work. Automated tests and deployment pipelines include security checks and controls so evidence of effective controls can be produced when needed.

guidance.text: Start by identifying where security currently enters the delivery process and where it is absent. Add an early security review for major features during design, before development work is considered ready to proceed. Invite InfoSec or security specialists to review upcoming designs and clarify common risks that developers should consider. Track the percentage of major features that receive an early security review.

Strengthen collaboration by making InfoSec participation part of regular delivery activities rather than an occasional checkpoint. Include security input during design discussions and software demos so feedback arrives while changes are still practical. Begin documenting security requirements for features in the same place as functional and testing requirements. Measure how much time security reviews add to development and look for avoidable delays in the review flow.

Expand security work into implementation by using preapproved libraries, packages, toolchains, and processes where applicable. Ask InfoSec to help define or review these approved resources so developers have clear options during daily work. Add automated checks that confirm approved components are used where required. Track both the availability and actual use of approved security-related tools and libraries.

Increase automation by adding security requirements to the automated testing process and continuous integration or build pipeline. Work with InfoSec to design tests for common vulnerabilities and for areas where approved code should be used. Review failed security checks as part of normal development work instead of deferring them to a release phase. Measure the number or percentage of security requirements covered by automated tests.

Sustain performance by keeping InfoSec involved across design, development, testing, demos, and release activities. Regularly update approved tools, libraries, and automated security tests as new risks or requirements are identified. Monitor whether security reviews remain early and whether their added time stays at an agreed minimum. Continue producing evidence from automated controls so audit and assessment needs do not depend on manual reconstruction.

name: Platform engineering
question: The team regularly uses a self-service internal platform that provides clear golden paths, automated workflows, actionable feedback, and standardized tools for building, testing, deploying, and troubleshooting applications without relying on manual infrastructure requests.
description: Platform engineering means treating shared delivery tooling as an internal product that supports software teams in their regular work. In practice, this capability shows up as self-service workflows, standardized toolchains, and golden paths that help teams build, test, deploy, and operate applications consistently. It reduces the need for developers to handle infrastructure, security, and operational complexity directly when completing common tasks. The platform also gives teams clear feedback, logs, and diagnostics so they can understand task outcomes and resolve problems with less dependence on specialist teams. The capability is visible when developers can follow reliable pathways for delivery work while still having enough flexibility for different team needs.

guidance.text: Start by identifying the most common delivery workflow that currently depends on manual tickets or specialist help. Map the steps developers take today, including where they wait, repeat work, or need infrastructure knowledge outside their main application responsibilities. Build a small self-service path for that workflow rather than attempting a complete platform. Measure whether developers can complete the workflow with fewer handoffs and clearer task outcomes.

Strengthen the initial platform path by turning repeated manual steps into automated, repeatable workflows. Add clear ownership for the platform as an internal product and gather feedback from developers using it in real project work. Focus on improving the most frequent tasks, such as creating services, deploying changes, or diagnosing failures. Track adoption and task success so the team can see whether the platform is replacing manual infrastructure requests.

Expand the platform beyond one workflow by defining golden paths for other high-value development and operations tasks. Standardize the tools, templates, and guardrails used across those paths while keeping the interface simple for application teams. Add actionable feedback, logs, and diagnostics so developers can understand failures and recover without waiting for a platform or operations team. Review delivery metrics and developer feedback together to confirm that platform use improves real work rather than adding hidden friction.

Design the platform so other teams can contribute tools and services through clear APIs, standards, and contribution rules. Reduce bottlenecks by avoiding a central platform team acting as the only source of every capability. Keep validating golden paths against developer journeys to ensure they fit actual workflows rather than assumed needs. Measure adoption, retention, task success, and delivery outcomes to detect where the platform still creates constraints or downstream disorder.

Sustain performance by continuously treating the platform as a product with active feedback loops, roadmap decisions, and usage measurement. Keep the platform focused on reducing cognitive load while preserving enough flexibility for different types of software teams. Monitor delivery performance, developer satisfaction, adoption, retention, and task success so regressions are visible early. Avoid regression by retiring unused paths, improving unclear feedback, and preventing the platform team from drifting back into reactive ticket handling.

name: Proactive failure notification
question: Team members receive actionable alerts from monitoring or logging systems before user-facing impact occurs, based on meaningful thresholds or rate-of-change signals, and these alerts reach people who can diagnose and address the issue.
description: Proactive failure notification means using monitoring and logging data to detect signs of trouble before a system has already failed. In practice, teams define alerting rules around metrics that are known to indicate upcoming user impact, such as threshold crossings or unusual rates of change. Notifications are routed to people or teams who are able to investigate and fix the problem. The capability is visible when alerts are timely, specific, actionable, and based on observed system health rather than customer reports or external escalation. It also includes reviewing incidents and alerts so that future notifications better predict real problems without overwhelming responders.

guidance.text: Start by identifying the most important services, user-facing functions, and failure modes that should not depend on customer reports or external escalation. Add basic monitoring for a small set of signals that indicate system health, such as errors, latency, saturation, or availability. Create initial alerting rules for conditions that clearly require human attention. Make each alert name the affected component, the signal that changed, and where responders should look first.

Strengthen alerting rules by connecting them to known failure thresholds rather than arbitrary metric values. Review recent incidents and support escalations to identify signals that appeared before impact became visible to users. Set alerts to trigger before those values are crossed, leaving time for diagnosis and response. Route each alert to the team or role that can actually take corrective action.

Expand monitoring beyond static thresholds by adding rate-of-change alerts for metrics that can become dangerous quickly. Compare alert activity with incidents to confirm whether notifications are early enough and specific enough to be useful. Remove or revise alerts that do not lead to action, because repeated non-actionable notifications reduce attention during real problems. Automate responses for notifications that always require the same action.

Use incident post-mortems to systematically capture which indicators could have predicted each failure earlier. Convert those indicators into new or adjusted alerts, including clear thresholds, time windows, and notification paths. Measure how often failures are first detected by internal monitoring versus customers, support, or external operations groups. Regularly review alert volume during incidents and tune notifications so responders receive useful signals rather than distracting noise.

Sustain the practice by treating alert rules, thresholds, routing, and automated responses as maintained parts of the service. Periodically verify that alerts still match current architecture, traffic patterns, and known user-impact thresholds. Track whether alerts remain actionable, timely, and routed to the people who can resolve the problem. Prevent regression by removing stale alerts, restoring disabled relevant alerts, and incorporating every significant incident into future monitoring improvements.

name: Streamlining change approval
question: To what extent do you agree that day-to-day change approvals are handled through timely peer review and automated checks, with high-risk changes identified early and clear approval steps for typical changes?
description: Streamlining change approval means structuring approval work so changes can move safely and efficiently through the delivery process. In practice, individual changes are reviewed by peers close to where the work happens, with review comments and approvals recorded in development tools. Automated testing, continuous integration, monitoring, and observability provide early feedback on defects, security, performance, and stability concerns. The process makes routine changes easier to approve while identifying higher-risk changes for additional attention. Team members understand the steps needed to move typical changes from submission to acceptance in a timely and repeatable way.

guidance.text: Start by mapping how changes currently move from submission to approval and release. Identify where approvals are delayed by external boards, unclear ownership, or missing evidence. Replace unclear approval expectations with a visible checklist that shows who reviews changes, where approval is captured, and what evidence is needed. Measure waiting time for approval so the team can see which steps create the largest delays.

Move approval activity closer to the development workflow by using peer review for individual changes. Capture review comments, approvals, and author separation directly in the development platform. Add or strengthen automated checks at code check-in so reviewers do not rely only on manual inspection. Track how often changes still wait for external approval and which types of changes are affected.

Strengthen automated detection of problems after changes are committed, including regressions, security issues, performance concerns, and stability signals. Review whether continuous testing, continuous integration, monitoring, and observability provide fast enough feedback for typical changes. Separate low-, medium-, and high-risk changes so routine work is not slowed by unnecessary approval steps. Measure both approval waiting time and change outcomes such as lead time and change failure rate.

Shift more validation into the development platform so developers receive fast feedback before approval becomes a bottleneck. Use ongoing analysis to identify high-risk changes early and route only those changes to additional scrutiny. Review the end-to-end change process regularly to find bottlenecks and remove manual checks that can be handled by tooling. Clarify the role of any change advisory group so it focuses on coordination, communication, and business trade-offs rather than detailed change review.

Sustain the process by continuing to measure approval waiting time, the percentage of changes needing manual approval, lead time, and change failure rate. Keep peer review, automated checks, and platform-level security controls aligned with the types of changes the team actually makes. Watch for regression into larger batches, added manual gates, or treating all changes as equally risky after production problems. Preserve clear communication of the process so team members consistently know how to move typical changes from submission to acceptance.

name: Team experimentation
question: Team members can independently explore new ideas, prototype and test solutions with users, and update stories or specifications during development when learning shows a better way to achieve agreed business outcomes.
description: Team experimentation means software teams have room to explore ideas while working toward agreed business outcomes or user problems. Team members can prototype, test, and learn from real users rather than only implementing fixed requirements. Stories and specifications are treated as adaptable descriptions of intent, not as unchangeable instructions. During development, teams can revise stories, specifications, tools, or technical approaches when new learning shows a better path. The capability is visible when teams make informed decisions inside the team and incorporate what they learn into the product or service.

guidance.text: Start by identifying where team members must ask for approval before exploring ideas or changing stories and specifications. Remove the most common approval step for low-risk changes that are still aligned with agreed business goals. Create a small, time-boxed space for prototyping or testing an idea with users. Track how often story or specification changes are blocked by permission requirements.

Strengthen the team’s access to business context, customer feedback, and the problem the work is meant to solve. Before implementation starts, clarify the intended outcome so developers can make informed choices during development. Allow story and specification changes when the team can explain how the change supports the outcome.
