Measure whether changes are happening during development and whether they are based on user or problem learning.

Make experimentation a normal part of delivery rather than a separate or occasional activity. Use quick prototypes, small tests, or direct user observation to check ideas before committing to larger solution choices. Give developers and operators opportunities to speak with or observe customers so decisions are grounded in real use. Review what was learned and explicitly fold that learning into stories, specifications, or product design.

Strengthen team autonomy by making most story, specification, and technical changes decidable within the team. Keep outside involvement focused on business goals, constraints, and shared outcomes rather than detailed implementation instructions. Encourage iteration when the first solution does not fully solve the problem or when later learning reveals a better option. Monitor whether permission requests are rare and whether experiments lead to changes in product or service design.

Sustain the practice by keeping teams connected to users, customer feedback, and organizational outcomes. Continue using small experiments, prototypes, and iterative improvements so experimentation remains part of everyday development. Watch for signs of regression, such as detailed external direction, rising approval requests, or stories becoming fixed instructions. Preserve team authority to change stories, specifications, tools, and technologies when informed learning shows a better way to achieve the agreed outcome.

name: Test automation
question: Team members create, maintain, and run fast, reliable automated unit and acceptance tests as part of normal development and delivery pipelines, using failures to identify real defects before release.
description: Test automation means that software changes are checked continuously by automated tests during development and delivery, rather than relying mainly on late manual regression testing. In practice, teams maintain fast and trustworthy unit and acceptance test suites that run locally and in continuous delivery pipelines. Developers are involved in writing and maintaining these tests, often alongside testers who contribute testing expertise and exploratory feedback. Test failures are treated as signals about product behavior or test design, so teams can detect and fix problems soon after changes are made. The capability is visible when automated tests support frequent feedback, releasable builds, and shared responsibility for quality across development and testing roles.

guidance.text: Start by creating a minimal automated test path that proves the delivery pipeline can run tests on each change. Add one useful unit test, one acceptance test for a high-value user flow, and an automated deployment step for a test environment. Stop treating testing as a separate phase after development is complete. Measure whether the pipeline runs these tests consistently and whether developers can see the results quickly.

Strengthen the suite by requiring automated tests for new or changed functionality, rather than trying to retrofit broad coverage all at once. Ask developers and testers to pair on acceptance tests so user behavior and code-level testability are both represented. Keep the first tests focused on important functionality and realistic user journeys. Track who writes and maintains the tests to ensure developers are becoming primary contributors.

Improve feedback speed by keeping automated tests fast enough for developers to run during normal work and for the continuous integration system to report results within minutes. Separate faster unit tests from slower acceptance tests so defects are caught as early and cheaply as possible. When an acceptance test or exploratory test finds a defect, add a lower-level test that would catch the same issue earlier next time. Measure the time spent fixing acceptance test failures and work to reduce it.

Strengthen trust in the test suite by removing or fixing flaky tests instead of accepting unreliable results. Review automated tests regularly to keep them well factored, maintainable, and aligned with current product behavior. Prune tests that are expensive to maintain, duplicated, or tightly coupled to implementation details without adding clear defect-detection value. Track how many test failures represent real product defects versus poorly written or unstable tests.

Sustain performance by continuously curating the automated test suite as the product, architecture, and user journeys change. Keep developers responsible for updating tests when code changes affect behavior, while maintaining tester involvement in exploratory testing, usability testing, and test design. Watch for regression signs such as slower feedback, broken pipelines, repeated flaky failures, or defects escaping to production. Use those signals to add, update, or remove tests so the pipeline remains a reliable indicator of releasability.

name: Test data management
question: To what extent do you agree that test data needed for automated tests is available on demand, relevant to the scenarios tested, isolated from other tests or processes, and does not delay or constrain test execution?
description: Test data management means ensuring that automated and manual tests have the data needed to exercise realistic, important, and unusual scenarios. In practice, teams keep test data available when tests need it, rather than depending on slow or fragile external sources. Effective test data is scoped to the tests that use it, kept current, and protected from unintended changes by other tests or processes. Teams also avoid unnecessary dependence on full production data, especially when it may be outdated, slow to refresh, or contain sensitive information. The capability is visible when test suites can run reliably, in full, and without test data becoming a bottleneck or constraint.

guidance.text: Start by identifying which automated tests cannot run because data is missing, outdated, unsafe, or controlled outside the test scope. Stop relying on unmanaged shared data or full production database copies as the default source for test execution. Select a small set of high-value user journeys, edge cases, and defect scenarios, then define the minimum data needed to test them. Track the time developers and testers spend preparing or repairing data so the constraint is visible.

Create a basic inventory of key test data sets and link each one to the automated tests or scenarios that require it. Strengthen test setup so tests create required state where practical, preferably through application APIs rather than manual database preparation. Replace stale or overly broad data extracts with smaller, relevant sections that are refreshed on a defined schedule. Measure which tests still require extra data work before they can run.

Improve isolation by ensuring data used by a specific test is clearly associated with that test and not modified by unrelated tests or processes. Reduce dependencies on durable database state by using locally stored data or in-memory databases where appropriate. Expand unit test coverage where tests can validate behavior without external data. Track how many automated tests can run without acquiring or manipulating additional data.

Make required test data consistently available on demand for full automated test suites. Regularly refresh relevant data sections and confirm that sensitive information is masked, hashed, or excluded. Review slow or brittle tests to determine whether database interaction, shared state, or excessive data setup is causing delays. Measure data set availability, access frequency, refresh frequency, and whether test data limits automated testing activities.

Sustain the practice by continuously checking that test data remains adequate, current, isolated, and safe as APIs and interfaces change. Keep the amount of maintained test data as small as possible while still covering common journeys, high-value scenarios, edge cases, defects, and error simulation. Monitor whether automated test suites can still run fully and in parallel without extra data preparation. Prevent regression by treating increased manual data work, stale extracts, sensitive unmasked data, or new shared-state dependencies as signals for immediate review.

name: Transformational leadership
question: Team leaders and influential team members consistently communicate a clear delivery vision, encourage new thinking about obstacles, consider personal needs, recognize useful improvements, and frame change as an opportunity in daily work.
description: Transformational leadership describes leadership behaviors that help software delivery teams adopt effective technical, product, and management practices. In practice, it means people in leadership roles communicate a clear direction, encourage constructive responses to change, and help team members rethink assumptions about their work. It also includes considering team members’ needs and recognizing improvements or valuable contributions in ways that reinforce useful behaviors. The capability is visible when these behaviors appear in everyday interactions, not only in formal management settings. DORA treats leadership as something that can be practiced by executives, managers, and other influential team members, especially when teams face pressure.

guidance.text: Start by making leadership behaviors visible in daily team interactions rather than treating leadership as only a management responsibility. Identify one current delivery goal and ask whether team members can describe it consistently without looking it up. Stop relying mainly on instructions, compliance, or punishment to influence behavior. Begin recognizing small examples of learning, collaboration, or improvement when they happen.

Strengthen communication around where the team is going and why current changes matter for delivery work. Ask team members what assumptions or past experiences are blocking adoption of useful practices, then discuss what could be tried differently this time. Make recognition more specific by linking praise to observable behaviors such as experimenting, helping another team, or improving work quality. Measure whether people can explain the team’s direction in similar terms.

Create regular opportunities for team members to examine obstacles to better delivery practices and propose alternatives. Use questions that surface lessons from previous attempts rather than simply approving or rejecting ideas. Strengthen supportive leadership by checking how decisions affect people’s needs, capacity, and ability to contribute. Track whether recognition is reinforcing behaviors that support technical, product, or management improvements.

Make the delivery vision more concrete by connecting it to measurable software delivery outcomes and the practices needed to reach them. Ensure inspirational communication is consistent during stressful periods, not only during planning or retrospectives. Encourage team members beyond formal managers to practice intellectual stimulation, support, and recognition in everyday collaboration. Review whether leadership behaviors are helping teams adopt the intended practices rather than becoming disconnected encouragement.

Sustain performance by keeping leadership behaviors consistent when delivery pressure, incidents, or organizational change create stress. Continue checking that the shared vision remains understood across the team without relying on documents or repeated reminders. Preserve recognition practices that reward learning, useful experimentation, and support for other teams, including when experiments do not succeed. Watch for regression into transactional patterns where compliance, status, or punishment becomes the main way to influence work.

name: Trunk-based development
question: Team members break work into small changes, integrate them into trunk daily, keep active branches short-lived, run automated tests before commit, and promptly fix or revert changes that break CI.
description: Trunk-based development means that developers integrate small, incremental code changes into the main code line frequently rather than allowing work to accumulate in long-lived branches. In practice, team members keep branches short-lived, merge to trunk at least daily, and limit the number of active branches in the application repository. Automated tests and CI checks are used around each commit so the main code line remains usable after changes are integrated. Code review and build processes are lightweight enough that changes can move into trunk without waiting for long approval or stabilization periods. Observable practices include frequent merges, few active branches, short-lived review cycles, working builds, and little or no need for code freezes.

guidance.text: Start by making current branching and integration behavior visible. Count active branches, branch age, merge frequency, code freeze periods, and time spent resolving merge conflicts. Identify one application repository where developers can begin splitting work into smaller changes and merging more frequently. Stop allowing large, long-lived branches to be the default path for normal feature work.

Strengthen the basic workflow by setting clear expectations for daily integration into trunk. Encourage developers to divide tasks into changes that can be reviewed, tested, and merged within a short working period. Add or improve automated tests that can run before code reaches trunk. Track whether branches and forks are actually merging at least once per day.

Focus on removing delays that prevent small changes from reaching trunk quickly. Review the code review process and reduce waiting time by using synchronous review or prioritizing review work when a change is ready. Make failed CI checks visible and require the responsible developer to fix the problem quickly or revert the change. Measure approval time for code changes and investigate outliers that slow integration.

Refine the system so frequent trunk integration is supported by fast feedback. Keep the build and test process short enough that developers can run meaningful checks before committing. Monitor active branch count, merge frequency, review delay, and code freeze time as regular team indicators. Use the data to address specific bottlenecks, such as slow tests, unstable builds, or branches that remain open too long.

Sustain the practice by continuously watching for drift toward long-lived branches, delayed reviews, or stabilization periods. Keep branch counts, merge frequency, build status, and review times visible so regressions are noticed early. Preserve fast automated testing and quick response to failed CI as normal team responsibilities. When new team members join or work patterns change, reinforce small-batch development and frequent integration as standard operating practices.

name: User-centric focus
question: The team regularly uses direct user feedback, user experience metrics, and observed user research to reprioritize work and validate that delivered software addresses real user needs rather than just increasing feature output.
description: User-centric focus means that software project teams keep the needs, experiences, and goals of users central to development decisions. In practice, teams gather feedback through direct channels, observe how users interact with the product, and use that evidence to shape priorities. They make user-oriented measures such as satisfaction, task completion, adoption, or retention visible alongside technical delivery metrics. Developers are connected to user research rather than receiving only filtered summaries, so user problems influence design, implementation, and testing choices. The capability is demonstrated when feedback and user outcomes regularly affect specifications, backlog decisions, and the evaluation of delivered software.

guidance.text: Start by creating at least one direct feedback channel that reaches the development team without long delays, such as in-app feedback, support review, or observed user sessions. Stop relying only on feature counts, velocity, or deployment activity to judge whether work is valuable. Select one user outcome metric, such as adoption, retention, satisfaction, or task completion, and review it during planning. Identify one recent feature and check whether any user need or feedback directly shaped it.

Strengthen the connection between feedback and prioritization by adding a regular step where user input is reviewed before backlog decisions are made. Make feedback visible to developers in its original or lightly summarized form so that important context is not lost. Track how often feedback leads to changes in specifications, priorities, or acceptance criteria. Stop treating user research as information owned only by product or UX roles.

Increase direct developer exposure to user problems by having engineers observe usability tests, user interviews, or real usage sessions on a recurring basis. Add user experience metrics to team dashboards beside technical measures, so delivery activity is interpreted together with user impact. Strengthen specifications by recording the user need, constraints, and expected outcome before implementation begins. Measure whether completed work changes adoption, satisfaction, retention, or task completion rather than only whether it was shipped.

Refine feedback loops so that user signals are available quickly enough to influence active planning and design decisions. Use observed behavior and product metrics to validate whether delivered changes solved the intended user problem. Strengthen spec-driven practices by keeping user needs and constraints as the reference point for implementation, testing, and any AI-assisted development. Stop allowing technology choices or model choices to drive work unless they are tied to a specific user problem.

Sustain the practice by routinely checking that feedback, user research, and user outcome metrics continue to influence prioritization and specifications. Monitor for regression into a feature-factory pattern by comparing output measures with adoption, retention, satisfaction, and task completion. Keep developers directly connected to users so that empathy and context remain part of design, coding, and validation work. For AI-assisted development, continue using user needs and constraints as the steering mechanism for prompts, generated code, and acceptance decisions.

name: Version control
question: Team members consistently store source code, dependencies, tests, build and deployment scripts, environment configurations, and AI-related artifacts in shared version control so changes can be traced, compared, restored, and reproduced reliably across environments.
description: Version control means that the files and artifacts needed to build, test, deploy, configure, and restore software are stored in a shared system that records their history. For software project teams, this includes application code, dependencies, automated tests, build and deployment scripts, infrastructure configuration, environment definitions, and relevant AI prompts or agent configuration files. The capability is visible when team members make small, traceable changes, can compare versions, and can recover or recreate previous working states from stored artifacts. It also supports reproducibility by allowing environments and deployable packages to be created from known versions of scripts and configuration. In practice, version control gives teams a reliable record of what changed, who changed it, and which versions were used in a given environment.

guidance.text: Start by identifying the essential artifacts that are not yet stored in version control, including source code, dependencies, tests, build scripts, deployment scripts, and environment configuration. Stop relying on local machines, shared drives, or undocumented manual steps as the primary record of delivery assets. Create a shared repository or set of repositories where these artifacts can be committed, reviewed, compared, and restored. Measure the percentage of critical delivery artifacts that are recoverable from version control.

Strengthen coverage beyond application code by adding database scripts, packaging scripts, infrastructure files, container definitions, cloud configuration, and deployment procedures. Make version control the default place where team members store changes that affect build, test, deployment, or environment creation. Start tagging or otherwise linking deployable versions to the artifacts and dependencies used to create them. Measure whether a team member can trace a deployment back to its source files, configuration, and dependency versions.

Improve reproducibility by ensuring packages and production-like test environments can be created using only artifacts stored in version control. Strengthen commit practices by keeping changes small enough to compare, review, merge, and revert without losing context. Add AI prompts and agent configuration files to version control when they influence development workflow or delivery behavior. Measure how quickly the team can recreate a deployable package or environment from the stored versioned assets.

Focus on making version control a reliable source for recovery, auditability, and environment comparison. Strengthen traceability by making it easy to determine which dependency, configuration, script, and application versions are present in any environment. Reduce practices that create hard-to-merge or hard-to-revert changes, such as long-lived branches or large delayed commits. Measure rollback success, merge conflict frequency, and the time required to compare two environment versions.

Sustain performance by regularly checking that all delivery-relevant artifacts remain versioned as systems, tools, and AI workflows evolve. Keep commits frequent and reviewable so the team retains a useful history for comparison, recovery, and audit. Periodically test whether build processes, preproduction environments, production-like environments, and recovery procedures can still be recreated from version-controlled assets. Watch for regression signals such as unversioned configuration changes, manual environment fixes, delayed commits, or artifacts stored outside the shared versioning approach.

name: Visibility of work in the value stream
question: Team members can easily see how product or feature work moves from initial business request through development, operations, and customer delivery, including current status, responsibilities, bottlenecks, and flow metrics such as lead time.
description: Visibility of work in the value stream means that teams can see how product or feature work flows from a business idea through delivery and use by customers. In practice, team members understand the major process steps, handoffs, responsible groups, current status, and how work is moving through the system. This capability is observable through value stream maps, card walls, storyboards, Kanban boards, or dashboards that show work items and their state. It also involves making flow information available, including measures such as lead time, process time, deployment frequency, and rework indicators like percent complete and accurate. The practical purpose is shared understanding of the full path of work rather than relying on isolated local views.

guidance.text: Start by making the team's current work visible in one shared place, such as a card wall, storyboard, Kanban board, or dashboard. Include each work item, its current state, and the next expected handoff so team members can see what is happening without relying on private status knowledge. Assign clear responsibility for keeping the display current and review it during regular team discussions.

Extend visibility beyond the team's immediate tasks by identifying the upstream sources of work and the downstream groups that receive it. Run a lightweight value stream mapping session with representatives from business, design, testing, QA, operations, and support where relevant. Capture the actual current process steps and handoffs rather than the process people assume is happening.

Add flow data to the visible process so the team can distinguish active work from waiting, rework, and delayed handoffs. Measure lead time, process time, and percent complete and accurate for the main process blocks in the value stream. Use these measurements to identify where work waits too long or where downstream teams frequently need rework.

Make value stream maps, workflow displays, responsibilities, and flow metrics available to everyone who contributes to or depends on the product development flow. Review the current-state map on a regular schedule and update it when the process changes. Create a future-state map with stakeholders and ensure the people involved have authority to act on agreed changes.

Sustain visibility by keeping maps, dashboards, and workflow displays current rather than treating them as one-time artifacts. Continue reviewing lead time, process time, deployment frequency, and percent complete and accurate to detect regression in flow or rework. Keep attention on constraints that affect the whole value stream, and avoid investing major effort in areas that do not improve end-to-end flow.

name: Visual management
question: Team members use current, easy-to-understand visual displays to track work status, delivery flow, build or system health, and actionable obstacles, and regularly act on the information during daily development work.
description: Visual management means making key information about software delivery work visible where team members can easily notice and use it. The information may show work in progress, build and pipeline status, delivery flow, production telemetry, or other signals tied to current team goals. In practice, the displays are simple enough to understand quickly, kept current during daily work, and focused on information the team can act on. They help create a shared view of process health and expose obstacles, defects, failures, or constraints that need attention. The capability is demonstrated when team members regularly use these displays to understand status, diagnose problems, and coordinate action rather than treating metrics as decorative reporting.

guidance.text: Start by identifying one or two system-level goals the team currently cares about and the information needed to understand progress toward them. Create a simple visual display, such as a board, chart, or monitor, that shows the current state and the desired state with only the precision needed for action. Stop relying on hidden reports, scattered tool views, or metrics chosen without team input. Review the display during daily work and note whether it reveals obstacles that team members can address.

Strengthen the connection between displayed information and daily decisions. Remove metrics that the team does not understand, cannot influence, or does not use when choosing work or responding to problems. Make the display easier to read at a glance by emphasizing the few signals that need attention now. Assign clear routines for keeping the information current so stale data does not become background noise.

Use the displays consistently to identify and remove obstacles, not just to report status. When a board, chart, build monitor, pipeline monitor, or telemetry view shows a problem, investigate the underlying cause instead of only restoring a favorable indicator. Add supporting diagnostic information only where it helps the team respond faster. In retrospectives, check whether people acted on the display and whether those actions contributed to measurable progress toward team goals.

Refine the visual management system as the team’s context and constraints change. Discard displays or metrics that no longer support current goals, and prototype new ones when new risks, bottlenecks, or system signals become important. Strengthen daily rituals around the display, such as selecting work, noticing blocked items, or swarming on failed builds and pipeline stages. Measure whether the displayed information remains accurate, actionable, and connected to improvements the team can observe.

Sustain performance by regularly reviewing whether each display still reflects information the team cares about and will act on. Keep the focus on solving the underlying problems revealed by the display rather than managing the metric itself. Avoid regression by checking for stale data, overloaded dashboards, copied displays from other teams, or signals that no longer match current goals. Continue adapting displays so they remain simple, current, relevant, and useful during daily work and retrospectives.

name: Well-being
question: To what extent do you agree that the project team keeps deployments low-stress, limits avoidable rework and defect-driven interruptions, and maintains work conditions that reduce overload, helplessness, and value conflicts?
description: Well-being reflects how software work affects people’s job satisfaction, stress, and ability to stay engaged over time. In practice, this capability is visible when deployments are routine rather than feared, and releasing code does not regularly create anxiety or disruption. It also shows up in how team time is spent, especially whether effort goes toward planned value-creating work instead of avoidable fixes, emergency patches, defects, and support caused by earlier quality gaps. The capability includes attention to burnout risks such as excessive workload, lack of control, weak support, unfair decisions, and conflicts between organizational and personal values. Together, these practices describe whether the work environment supports sustainable software delivery rather than relying on individuals to absorb chronic stress.

guidance.text: Start by making the sources of stress and waste visible. Track how often deployments cause anxiety, disruption, emergency follow-up, or unplanned fixes. Separate planned new work from rework, break/fix activity, customer-support work, defects found by users, and security remediation. Identify the most common patterns that cause overload, helplessness, or repeated urgent work.

Strengthen the practices that reduce disruptive deployments and prevent issues from being passed downstream. Review recent incidents, defects, emergency patches, and support escalations to identify where quality was not built in early enough. Reduce avoidable rework by improving checks before release and clarifying ownership for fixing recurring causes. Watch for workload levels that regularly exceed reasonable limits and make those constraints explicit in planning.

Build a more consistent balance between planned value-creating work and reactive work. Measure how much team time is spent on new features, tests, infrastructure, unplanned work, defect correction, security remediation, and customer support. Use those measures to decide where deployment practices or quality practices need strengthening. Increase team control by involving the people doing the work in decisions that affect delivery, release timing, and remediation priorities.

Focus on reducing the remaining causes of deployment pain, rework, and burnout risk. Examine whether urgent work still clusters around particular systems, release paths, defect types, or decision processes. Strengthen supportive team routines so problems are handled as work-environment issues rather than as individual endurance problems. Check whether recognition, fairness, community, and value alignment are reflected in everyday decisions about priorities, workload, and support.

Sustain performance by continuing to measure deployment pain, rework, unplanned work, defect-driven work, and burnout risk factors over time. Do not treat low stress or low rework as permanent; review trends after major delivery changes, staffing changes, or increases in workload. Preserve team control, fairness, community support, and value alignment in planning and decision-making. Act quickly when deployments become disruptive again or when reactive work begins displacing planned value-creating work.

name: Work in process limits
question: To what extent do you agree that work items across the software delivery flow are visible, prioritized with stakeholders, limited by team capacity at each stage, and pulled forward only when space is available rather than starting more work?
description: Work in process limits is the practice of making all active work visible and capping how many items can be in each stage of the delivery process at the same time. For software project teams, it means work is prioritized, represented on a shared board, and moved through analysis, development, testing, operations, and delivery as capacity becomes available. The capability emphasizes finishing a small number of high-priority items before starting more work, rather than spreading people across many simultaneous tasks. Observable practices include explicit limits per workflow column, pull-based movement of the highest priority item, and attention to blocked or aging work. It also treats lead time and variability across the whole value stream as signals of how smoothly work is flowing from idea to customer.

guidance.text: Start by creating a single visible board for all work currently in progress, including analysis, development, testing, operations, production support, and technical debt. Add start dates or daily aging markers so blocked or long-running items can be seen. Hold a short prioritization discussion with stakeholders to order the visible work before new items are started. The immediate goal is to make hidden inventory and overload visible enough to set initial limits.

Set explicit limits for each workflow stage based on the capacity of the people who can actually work there. Account for recurring obligations such as support, meetings, and technical debt when deciding those limits. Do not allow more active items in a stage than the available capacity can handle. When a stage reaches its limit, pull the highest priority waiting item only after another item moves forward.

Strengthen adherence to the limits when pressure rises or when some people appear idle. Avoid increasing limits just to keep everyone busy, because that hides constraints and increases multitasking. Redirect available people toward the blocked part of the value stream or the team that is creating the delay. Record start and end dates for work items so lead time can be reviewed during retrospectives.

Use lead time and variability across the full path from idea to customer to identify where flow is unstable. In retrospectives, review whether WIP limits are surfacing obstacles and whether concrete actions are being taken on those obstacles. Reduce unnecessary hand-offs, simplify or automate repeated tasks, and strengthen collaboration around constrained stages. When current limits become easy to maintain, lower them carefully to expose the next barrier to flow.

Keep WIP limits explicit, visible, and connected to actual team capacity rather than allowing them to drift over time. Continue checking for hidden work, oversized limits, too many workflow columns, and people splitting time across multiple tasks or projects. Maintain regular stakeholder prioritization so the next pulled item reflects the current highest priority. Sustain performance by measuring lead time and variability, acting on newly exposed constraints, and preserving the pull-based flow even during periods of delivery pressure.

name: Working in small batches
question: Software changes are routinely split into small, independently testable work items that can be completed quickly, integrated into trunk, released or dark launched without waiting for large feature bundles, and validated through timely feedback.
description: Working in small batches means dividing software work into small, independent changes that can be completed, tested, integrated, and released quickly. This capability helps teams get feedback sooner from users, automated tests, monitoring, quality assurance, and production behavior. In practice, it is visible when features are sliced into increments that take hours or a few days, rather than large bundles that wait for a later release. Teams applying this capability check in small releasable changes frequently, avoid long-lived feature branches, and use approaches such as dark launching or feature toggles to release incomplete larger features safely. The central intent is to reduce delay between making a change and learning whether it works as expected and provides value.

guidance.text: Start by identifying where work is currently too large to test, integrate, or release quickly. Break the next planned feature into smaller independent slices before development begins, using criteria such as value, testability, and independence. Stop allowing work to accumulate until an entire feature or release is complete before downstream testing starts. Measure how long typical work items take from start to production feedback so the team can see the current batch size clearly.

Strengthen planning by making every new work item small enough to complete in days rather than weeks. Ask whether each slice can be tested, monitored, and verified separately, and revise items that depend on large bundled delivery. Start committing usable increments even when the full feature is not yet visible to users. Track which items cannot be completed within one week and examine why they resisted decomposition.

Move from occasional small slicing to a consistent development habit. Encourage developers to check small releasable changes into trunk frequently, ideally at least once per day when active development is underway. Use automated tests, monitoring, and quality checks to verify each slice quickly after integration. Stop regrouping completed small batches into larger downstream packages for testing or release, because that delays feedback.

Strengthen the release path so small changes can reach production or production-like validation without waiting for complete features. Use dark launching, feature toggles, or branch by abstraction when larger changes must be built incrementally while keeping trunk releasable. Start development at service or API boundaries when that allows safe production deployment before user-visible UI changes are complete. Measure whether production delays are linked to work items that were still too large.

Sustain the practice by regularly reviewing batch size, release frequency, and feedback speed across the team. Keep checking that features are decomposed into independently testable and valuable slices rather than drifting back toward large pull requests or long-lived branches. When using AI coding assistants, maintain discipline around small, reviewable changes instead of accepting large generated blocks without careful decomposition. Continue using feedback from users, monitoring, automated tests, and quality assurance to validate each batch soon after release.
